title: Advancing Innovation: Technology Transitions and Early Investments
description: Collaborating to turn scientific breakthroughs into equitable economic opportunities
text:
Research is at the heart of technological advancements The Department of Energy works collaboratively to ensure that innovative energy technologies move through the critical stages of research, development, and commercialization.
The Office of Science supports use-inspired basic research through individual projects as well as multi-institution collaborations like our Energy Frontier Research Centers and Bioenergy Research Centers. In addition, the Office of Science provides user facilities such as X-ray light sources and neutron sources that allow research institutions and private companies to conduct both discovery and applied research. Building on these discoveries, the Department’s applied research programs narrow the focus to address specific societal needs such as clean energy and a more efficient electrical grid. The Office of Technology Transitions (OTT) plays a key role in facilitating this process, working together with the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, the Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management, the Office of Nuclear Energy, the Office of Electricity, the Office of Clean Energy Demonstrations, and others to catalyze commercialization support, funding, and programming to unlock early investments and technology transitions.
By working together across a range of programs and initiatives, DOE helps ensure that the energy technologies of tomorrow can secure private capital, find customers, grow revenues, and succeed in the marketplace to meet the needs of today’s energy challenges.
The U.S. Department of Energy’s (DOE) Lab-Embedded Entrepreneurship Program (LEEP) recruits the best and brightest minds from across the clean and renewable energy sector through a national call for a two-year funded fellowship. The primary purpose of this fellowship is to provide fellows who have early-stage clean energy startups and/or emerging technologies with the support needed to develop and transition their ideas into the market.
These entrepreneurs are embedded for a period of two years at one of four national labs where they are mentored by a lab scientist. In addition, LEEP also provides support at the local, regional, and national levels including entrepreneurship training and a networking ecosystem to eliminate the hurdles traditionally faced by early-stage cleantech startups.
